content.body: All matter is made of atoms. An atom is the smallest particle of an element that retains the chemical properties of that element. Atoms are incredibly small — a single full stop on this page contains about 250 billion atoms.

Structure of an atom:
• Nucleus (centre) — contains protons and neutrons
• Electron shells (outside) — contain electrons

Proton: positive charge (+1), mass = 1 atomic mass unit
Neutron: no charge (0), mass = 1 atomic mass unit
Electron: negative charge (−1), mass = almost 0 (negligible)

content.title: Atomic Number and Mass Number
content.body:
Atomic Number (Z) = number of protons in the nucleus
Mass Number (A) = number of protons + number of neutrons
Number of neutrons = Mass Number − Atomic Number

Example — Carbon:
Atomic number = 6 (6 protons)
Mass number = 12
Neutrons = 12 − 6 = 6

In a neutral (uncharged) atom, the number of electrons equals the number of protons. This is because the positive charges of protons exactly cancel the negative charges of electrons, making the atom overall electrically neutral.

content.title: Electron Arrangement
content.body:
Electrons occupy shells (energy levels) around the nucleus:
Shell 1 (closest to nucleus): maximum 2 electrons
Shell 2: maximum 8 electrons
Shell 3: maximum 8 electrons (for first 20 elements)

Example: Sodium (Na), atomic number 11
Electron arrangement: 2, 8, 1
(2 in shell 1, 8 in shell 2, 1 in shell 3)

questions.question: What particles are found in the nucleus?
questions.explanation: The nucleus of an atom contains protons (positive charge) and neutrons (no charge). Electrons orbit the nucleus in shells.

questions.question: What is the charge of a proton?
questions.explanation: A proton has a positive charge of +1.

questions.question: Carbon has atomic number 6 and mass number 12. How many neutrons does it have?
questions.explanation: Neutrons = Mass number − Atomic number = 12 − 6 = 6

questions.question: In a neutral atom, what equals the number of protons?
questions.explanation: In a neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the number of protons so the positive and negative charges cancel out.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of electrons in the first shell?
questions.explanation: The first electron shell (closest to the nucleus) can hold a maximum of 2 electrons.

questions.question: Sodium has atomic number 11. What is its electron arrangement?
questions.explanation: Sodium has 11 electrons arranged as: 2 in shell 1, 8 in shell 2, 1 in shell 3.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of an element?
questions.explanation: The atomic number (Z) is the number of protons in the nucleus. It uniquely identifies each element.

questions.question: Which particle has negligible (almost zero) mass?
questions.explanation: Electrons are about 1836 times lighter than protons or neutrons. Their mass is so small it is considered negligible.

questions.question: Oxygen has atomic number 8 and mass number 16. How many neutrons does it have?
questions.explanation: Neutrons = Mass number − Atomic number = 16 − 8 = 8

questions.question: What is the maximum number of electrons in the second shell?
questions.explanation: The second electron shell can hold a maximum of 8 electrons.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: The Periodic Table is a systematic arrangement of all known elements in order of increasing atomic number. It was originally developed by Dmitri Mendeleev in 1869, who arranged elements by atomic mass and noticed repeating patterns in their properties.

The modern Periodic Table:
• 118 elements known so far
• Arranged in order of atomic number (left to right)
• 7 horizontal rows called PERIODS
• 18 vertical columns called GROUPS
• Elements in the same group have similar chemical properties

content.title: Periods and Groups
content.body:
PERIOD = horizontal row
Period 1: H, He (2 elements)
Period 2: Li to Ne (8 elements)
Period 3: Na to Ar (8 elements)

GROUP = vertical column
Group I (Alkali Metals): Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr
Group VII (Halogens): F, Cl, Br, I
Group 0 (Noble Gases): He, Ne, Ar, Kr

content.title: Key Regions of the Periodic Table
content.body:
Metals — left side and middle (e.g. Na, Fe, Cu, Au)
Non-metals — upper right (e.g. C, N, O, S, Cl)
Metalloids — between metals and non-metals (e.g. Si, Ge)
Transition metals — middle block (Groups 3-12)

The group number tells you how many electrons are in the outer shell. The period number tells you how many electron shells the atom has. This is why elements in the same group react similarly — they have the same number of outer electrons.

content.title: Reading the Periodic Table
content.body:
Example: Sodium (Na)
Atomic number: 11 → 11 protons, 11 electrons
Period 3 → 3 electron shells
Group I → 1 outer electron
Electron arrangement: 2, 8, 1

questions.question: Who developed the first Periodic Table?
questions.explanation: Dmitri Mendeleev (1869) arranged elements by atomic mass and noticed repeating patterns, creating the first Periodic Table.

questions.question: What are the horizontal rows in the Periodic Table called?
questions.explanation: Horizontal rows are called Periods. There are 7 periods in the modern Periodic Table.

questions.question: What are the vertical columns in the Periodic Table called?
questions.explanation: Vertical columns are called Groups. Elements in the same group have similar chemical properties.

questions.question: Elements in the same group have similar properties because they have the same...
questions.explanation: Elements in the same group have the same number of electrons in their outermost shell, giving them similar chemical behaviour.

questions.question: What does the period number tell you about an atom?
questions.explanation: The period number equals the number of electron shells. Period 3 elements have 3 shells.

questions.question: Which group contains the Noble Gases?
questions.explanation: Noble Gases (He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn) are in Group 0 (also called Group 18). They are unreactive due to full outer shells.

questions.question: Which group contains the Alkali Metals?
questions.explanation: Group I (Alkali Metals) includes Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs. They all have 1 outer electron and react vigorously with water.

questions.question: Where are most metals found in the Periodic Table?
questions.explanation: Metals are located on the left side and in the centre (transition metals) of the Periodic Table.

questions.question: Chlorine is in Period 3, Group VII. How many electron shells does it have?
questions.explanation: The period number tells us the number of electron shells. Chlorine is in Period 3, so it has 3 electron shells.

questions.question: How many elements are in Period 2 of the Periodic Table?
questions.explanation: Period 2 contains 8 elements: Li, Be, B, C, N, O, F, Ne.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons. They have the same atomic number but different mass numbers. Since they have the same number of protons and electrons, isotopes have identical chemical properties.

content.title: Isotopes of Carbon
content.body:
Carbon-12: 6 protons, 6 neutrons (most common, 98.9%)
Carbon-13: 6 protons, 7 neutrons (about 1.1%)
Carbon-14: 6 protons, 8 neutrons (radioactive, used in carbon dating)

All three are carbon because they all have 6 protons.
They differ only in mass (number of neutrons).

content.title: Isotopes of Chlorine
content.body:
Chlorine-35: 17 protons, 18 neutrons (75% abundance)
Chlorine-37: 17 protons, 20 neutrons (25% abundance)

This is why the relative atomic mass of chlorine is 35.5 — it is the average of its isotopes weighted by abundance.

Relative Atomic Mass (Ar):
Ar = (mass of isotope 1 × % abundance + mass of isotope 2 × % abundance) ÷ 100

For Chlorine:
Ar = (35 × 75 + 37 × 25) ÷ 100
Ar = (2625 + 925) ÷ 100 = 3550 ÷ 100 = 35.5

Some isotopes are radioactive — their nuclei are unstable and decay over time, releasing radiation. Carbon-14 is radioactive and is used in carbon dating to determine the age of ancient organic materials. Uranium-235 is used as nuclear fuel in power stations.

questions.question: What are isotopes?
options: Atoms with same protons but different neutrons
Atoms with same neutrons but different protons
questions.answer: Atoms with same protons but different neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same atomic number but different mass numbers due to different neutron counts.

questions.question: Carbon-12 and Carbon-14 are isotopes. What do they have in common?
options: Same number of electrons and neutrons
questions.explanation: All carbon isotopes have 6 protons (atomic number 6). They differ in their number of neutrons.

questions.question: Why do isotopes have the same chemical properties?
options: Same number of protons and electrons
questions.answer: Same number of protons and electrons
questions.explanation: Chemical properties depend on electron arrangement. Isotopes have the same number of electrons, so they behave identically in chemical reactions.

questions.question: Chlorine has two isotopes: Cl-35 (75%) and Cl-37 (25%). What is its relative atomic mass?
Carbon-14 is used for...
questions.explanation: Carbon-14 is a radioactive isotope that decays at a known rate. Scientists measure how much remains to determine the age of ancient organic materials.

questions.question: How many neutrons does Carbon-14 have?
questions.explanation: Carbon-14 has atomic number 6 (6 protons) and mass number 14. Neutrons = 14 − 6 = 8.

questions.question: An element has two isotopes: one with mass 10 (20%) and one with mass 11 (80%). What is the relative atomic mass?
Uranium-235 is mainly used as...
questions.explanation: Uranium-235 is a fissile isotope that splits to release enormous energy. It is used as fuel in nuclear power stations.

questions.question: Chlorine-37 has 17 protons. How many neutrons does it have?
questions.explanation: Neutrons = Mass number − Atomic number = 37 − 17 = 20

questions.question: Why is the relative atomic mass of chlorine 35.5 and not a whole number?
options: It is an average of its isotopes weighted by their abundance
questions.answer: It is an average of its isotopes weighted by their abundance
questions.explanation: Relative atomic mass is a weighted average. Chlorine is 75% Cl-35 and 25% Cl-37, giving an average of 35.5.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: An ion is a charged particle formed when an atom gains or loses electrons. Metals tend to lose electrons to form positive ions (cations). Non-metals tend to gain electrons to form negative ions (anions).

Ion formation:
Metal loses electrons → positive ion (cation)
Example: Na → Na⁺ + e⁻ (sodium loses 1 electron)

Non-metal gains electrons → negative ion (anion)
Example: Cl + e⁻ → Cl⁻ (chlorine gains 1 electron)

content.title: Common Ions You Must Know
content.body:
Na⁺ (sodium ion) — loses 1 electron
K⁺ (potassium ion) — loses 1 electron
Ca²⁺ (calcium ion) — loses 2 electrons
Al³⁺ (aluminium ion) — loses 3 electrons
Cl⁻ (chloride ion) — gains 1 electron
O²⁻ (oxide ion) — gains 2 electrons
SO₄²⁻ (sulphate ion)
NO₃⁻ (nitrate ion)
OH⁻ (hydroxide ion)

content.title: Writing Chemical Formulae
content.body:
The formula of an ionic compound is written so the total charges balance to zero.

Example 1: Sodium chloride
Na⁺ and Cl⁻ → charges are +1 and −1 → they balance → NaCl

Example 2: Calcium chloride
Ca²⁺ and Cl⁻ → need 2 Cl⁻ to balance Ca²⁺ → CaCl₂

Example 3: Aluminium oxide
Al³⁺ and O²⁻ → need 2 Al³⁺ and 3 O²⁻ → Al₂O₃

Cross-multiply method:
For compound XᵃYᵇ:
The charge on X becomes the subscript of Y
The charge on Y becomes the subscript of X

Example: Al³⁺ and O²⁻ → Al₂O₃
(3 becomes subscript of O, 2 becomes subscript of Al)

questions.question: What is an ion?
options: A charged particle formed by gaining or losing electrons
questions.answer: A charged particle formed by gaining or losing electrons
questions.explanation: An ion is an atom or group of atoms that has gained or lost electrons, giving it an overall positive or negative charge.

questions.question: What type of ion do metals form?
questions.explanation: Metals lose electrons to form positive ions (cations). For example, sodium loses 1 electron to form Na⁺.

questions.question: What is the formula for calcium chloride?
questions.explanation: Calcium is Ca²⁺ and chloride is Cl⁻. Two Cl⁻ ions are needed to balance one Ca²⁺. So the formula is CaCl₂.

questions.question: How many electrons does Al³⁺ lose compared to a neutral aluminium atom?
questions.explanation: Al³⁺ has a charge of +3, meaning it has lost 3 electrons from neutral aluminium (which has 13 protons and 13 electrons).

questions.question: What is the formula for aluminium oxide?
questions.explanation: Al³⁺ and O²⁻: using cross-multiply method, charge 3 → subscript for O, charge 2 → subscript for Al. Formula: Al₂O₃.

questions.question: Chloride ion (Cl⁻) is formed when chlorine...
questions.explanation: Chlorine gains 1 electron to achieve a full outer shell, forming the chloride ion Cl⁻ with charge −1.

questions.question: What is the charge on the sulphate ion?
questions.explanation: The sulphate ion is SO₄²⁻, with a charge of −2.

questions.question: What is the formula for sodium oxide?
questions.explanation: Na⁺ and O²⁻: two Na⁺ are needed to balance one O²⁻. Formula: Na₂O.

questions.question: Which of these is a negative ion?
questions.explanation: Cl⁻ (chloride) is a negative ion (anion). The others — Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Al³⁺ — are all positive ions (cations).

questions.question: What is the formula for calcium oxide?
questions.explanation: Ca²⁺ and O²⁻ both have charge of magnitude 2. They balance in a 1:1 ratio. Formula: CaO.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

lessons.title: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

content.body:
Atomic structure:
Proton: +1 charge, mass 1 amu, in nucleus
Neutron: 0 charge, mass 1 amu, in nucleus
Electron: -1 charge, mass ~0, orbits nucleus

Atomic number Z = number of protons
Mass number A = protons + neutrons
Neutrons = A - Z
In neutral atom: electrons = protons

Electronic configuration: shells 2, 8, 8, 18...
Period = number of occupied shells
Group = number of valence electrons

content.title: Reading Periodic Table
content.body:
₁₁²³Na: Z=11, A=23, neutrons=12
Configuration: 2,8,1 → Period 3, Group 1

₈¹⁶O: Z=8, neutrons=8
Configuration: 2,6 → Period 2, Group 6

Group 1: alkali metals (1 valence e⁻)
Group 7: halogens (7 valence e⁻)
Group 0/18: noble gases (full outer shell)

content.title: Isotopes
content.body:
Same element (Z), different A (different neutrons)
¹H, ²H (deuterium), ³H (tritium)
¹²C (98.9%), ¹³C (1.1%), ¹⁴C (radioactive)

Relative atomic mass = weighted average of isotope masses
Cl: 75% ³⁵Cl + 25% ³⁷Cl
Ar = 0.75×35 + 0.25×37 = 35.5

questions.question: Number of neutrons in ₁₇³⁵Cl
questions.explanation: Neutrons = A - Z = 35 - 17 = 18

questions.question: Element with Z=8 is in which group?
questions.explanation: O: Z=8, config 2,6 → 6 outer electrons → Group 6 (or Group 16 in IUPAC)

questions.question: Isotopes differ in
questions.explanation: Isotopes: same Z (same element) but different N (different mass)

questions.question: Electronic configuration of Na (Z=11)
questions.explanation: Na: 2 in K shell, 8 in L shell, 1 in M shell

questions.question: An atom has 2,8,7 electrons. Period and Group?
questions.explanation: 3 shells = Period 3; 7 outer electrons = Group 7 (halogens)

questions.question: Relative atomic mass of element with 60% mass 10 and 40% mass 11 =
Noble gases have full outer shells so they
questions.explanation: Full outer shell = maximum stability. Noble gases rarely react (though Xe, Kr can form some compounds)

questions.question: Ion ₈O²⁻ has how many electrons?
questions.explanation: O has 8 protons. O²⁻ gained 2 electrons: 8+2=10 electrons

questions.question: In period 3 (Na to Ar), moving left to right metallic character
questions.explanation: Left side: metals (Na, Mg, Al). Right side: non-metals (P, S, Cl). Metallic character decreases across period

questions.question: Mass spectrometer measures
questions.explanation: Mass spec ionises atoms, accelerates them, deflects in magnetic field — separation by m/z ratio

lessons.title: Atoms and the Periodic Table — Advanced Part 6

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Atoms and the Periodic Table. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Atoms and the Periodic Table concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Atoms and the Periodic Table builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Atoms and the Periodic Table. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Atoms and the Periodic Table. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Atoms and the Periodic Table include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Atoms and the Periodic Table. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
